text: Active recovery clears accumulated blood lactate faster than passive recovery after high-intensity running.

Blood lactate clearance during active recovery is intensity-dependent, with higher intensities (60-100% of lactate threshold) clearing lactate more rapidly than lower intensities (0-40% of lactate threshold).
Maximum blood lactate clearance occurs at active recovery intensities close to the lactate threshold (80-100% of lactate threshold).
Active recovery at 80-100% of lactate threshold produces shorter time constants for 67% lactate clearance and higher peak clearance rates compared to 40% of lactate threshold or passive recovery.
Running at 90% of VO2max for 5-minute bouts increases blood lactate concentration from approximately 1.0 to 3.9 mmol/L.
Self-regulated higher-intensity recovery produces enhanced lactate clearance compared to self-regulated lower-intensity recovery, confirming the intensity dependence of lactate clearance.
